title: Peripheral Neuropathy
authors: Anthony A. Amato, Richard J. Barohn

items.title: Approach to Diagnosing Peripheral Neuropathy: Identifying Lesion Location, Cause, and Treatment
items.content: The clinical approach to diagnosing peripheral neuropathy involves three main goals: identifying the lesion location, determining the cause, and deciding on proper treatment. This is achieved through a thorough history, neurologic examination, electrodiagnostic studies, and other laboratory tests.
keyPoints: Peripheral nerves consist of sensory, motor, and autonomic elements.
Neuropathies can affect cell bodies or peripheral processes (axons/myelin).
Motor axons are large myelinated fibers conducting at ~50 m/s.
Sensory fibers include large-diameter for proprioception/vibration and small myelinated/unmyelinated for pain/temperature.
Peripheral neuropathies can impair sensory, motor, or autonomic function singly or in combination.
Classified into neuronopathy/ganglionopathy (cell body), myelinopathy (myelin), and axonopathy (axon).
Approximately 50% of cases remain idiopathic/cryptogenic despite extensive evaluation.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · Peripheral Neuropathy | GENERAL APPROACH

items.title: Understanding the Classification and Clinical Features of Peripheral Neuropathies
items.content: Peripheral neuropathies are classified based on their primary site of pathology: neuronopathy/ganglionopathy (cell body), myelinopathy (myelin sheaths), and axonopathy (axons). Each class has distinct clinical and electrophysiologic features, aiding in diagnosis.
keyPoints: Peripheral nerves contain sensory, motor, and autonomic fibers.
Neuropathies can affect cell bodies or peripheral processes.
Motor axons are large myelinated fibers (~50 m/s).
Sensory fibers vary: large for proprioception/vibration; small for pain/temperature.
Autonomic nerves are small in diameter.
Lesions may impair sensory, motor, or autonomic function.
Neuropathies classified into neuronopathy/ganglionopathy (cell body), myelinopathy (myelin), and axonopathy (axon).
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · Peripheral Neuropathy | GENERAL APPROACH

items.subtopic: INFORMATION FROM THE HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: SEVEN KEY QUESTIONS (TABLE 457-1) | TABLE 457-1 Approach to Neuropathic Disorders: Seven Key Questions
items.title: Identifying Motor, Sensory, and Autonomic Involvement in Peripheral Neuropathy
items.content: Determining which systems are involved—motor, sensory, autonomic, or a combination—is crucial for diagnosing neuropathies. Weakness without sensory or autonomic dysfunction suggests motor neuropathy, neuromuscular junction abnormalities, or myopathy. Autonomic involvement may indicate amyloid polyneuropathy if present in non-diabetic patients.
keyPoints: Motor symptoms alone suggest motor neuropathy, neuromuscular junction abnormality, or myopathy.
Autonomic dysfunction without diabetes suggests possible amyloid polyneuropathy.
Most neuropathies are predominantly sensory.

items.subtopic: INFORMATION FROM THE HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: SEVEN KEY QUESTIONS (TABLE 457-1) | TABLE 457-1 Approach to Neuropathic Disorders: Seven Key Questions
items.title: Assessing the Distribution of Weakness in Peripheral Neuropathy
items.content: The pattern and distribution of weakness are key diagnostic indicators. Symmetric proximal and distal weakness often points to acquired immune demyelinating polyneuropathies like Guillain-Barré syndrome or chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy. Asymmetric or multifocal weakness suggests other conditions such as motor neuron disease.
keyPoints: Symmetric proximal and distal weakness indicates possible GBS or CIDP.
Asymmetric or multifocal weakness may suggest motor neuron disease like ALS.
Exclude treatable conditions like multifocal motor neuropathy.

items.subtopic: INFORMATION FROM THE HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: SEVEN KEY QUESTIONS (TABLE 457-1) | TABLE 457-1 Approach to Neuropathic Disorders: Seven Key Questions
items.title: Characterizing Sensory Involvement in Peripheral Neuropathy
items.content: Understanding the nature of sensory involvement helps narrow differential diagnoses. Small-fiber neuropathy is suggested by loss of pain and temperature perception with preserved vibratory and position sense, often linked to diabetes mellitus or idiopathic causes.
keyPoints: Small-fiber neuropathy presents as loss of pain/temperature sensation but preserved vibratory/position sense.
Common causes include diabetes mellitus or glucose intolerance.
Amyloid neuropathy is a consideration in small-fiber neuropathies.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · INFORMATION FROM THE HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: SEVEN KEY QUESTIONS (TABLE 457-1) | TABLE 457-1 Approach to Neuropathic Disorders: Seven Key Questions

items.title: Identifying Sensory Neuronopathy/Ganglionopathy
items.content: A neurologic examination revealing a dramatic loss of proprioception and vibration with normal strength should prompt consideration of sensory neuronopathy/ganglionopathy. This is particularly suggested if the loss is asymmetric or affects the arms more than the legs, indicating a non-length-dependent process.
keyPoints: Dramatic loss of proprioception and vibration
Normal muscle strength
Asymmetric involvement or greater arm involvement suggests sensory neuronopathy/ganglionopathy
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · TABLE 457-2 Patterns of Neuropathic Disorders

items.title: Recognizing Combined System Degeneration with Neuropathy
items.content: In patients presenting with symmetric distal sensory symptoms and signs suggestive of a distal sensory neuropathy, additional evidence of symmetric upper motor neuron involvement should lead to consideration of combined system degeneration. Vitamin B12 deficiency is the most common cause, but other etiologies like copper deficiency, HIV infection, severe hepatic disease, adrenomyeloneuropathy (AMN), and hereditary spastic paraplegia with neuropathy should also be considered.
keyPoints: Symmetric distal sensory symptoms
Evidence of symmetric upper motor neuron involvement
Common cause: Vitamin B12 deficiency
Other etiologies: Copper deficiency, HIV, severe hepatic disease, AMN, hereditary spastic paraplegia with neuropathy
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · TABLE 457-2 Patterns of Neuropathic Disorders

items.subtopic: PATTERN RECOGNITION APPROACH TO NEUROPATHIC DISORDERS | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES
items.title: How Electrodiagnostic Studies Aid in Classifying Neuropathies
items.content: Electrodiagnostic studies, including nerve conduction studies (NCS) and needle electromyography (EMG), are crucial for classifying neuropathic disorders. They help determine whether the disorder is a mononeuropathy, multiple mononeuropathy, radiculopathy, plexopathy, or generalized polyneuropathy. Additionally, they identify if sensory, motor, autonomic fibers, or combinations thereof are involved and distinguish between axonopathies and myelinopathies.
keyPoints: NCS and EMG help classify neuropathies into specific patterns: mononeuropathy, multiple mononeuropathy, radiculopathy, plexopathy, generalized polyneuropathy.
Electrophysiologic data can distinguish between axonopathies and myelinopathies.
NCS are useful in differentiating axonal degeneration from segmental demyelination.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · PATTERN RECOGNITION APPROACH TO NEUROPATHIC DISORDERS | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES

items.subtopic: PATTERN RECOGNITION APPROACH TO NEUROPATHIC DISORDERS | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES
items.title: Distinguishing Axonal Degeneration from Demyelinating Neuropathies Using NCS
items.content: Nerve conduction studies (NCS) are instrumental in differentiating between axonal degeneration and demyelination. Low-amplitude potentials with preserved distal latencies, conduction velocities, and late potentials suggest an axonal neuropathy. Conversely, slow conduction velocities, prolonged distal latencies, and the absence of fibrillations on EMG indicate a primary demyelinating neuropathy.
keyPoints: Low-amplitude potentials with preserved distal latencies and conduction velocities suggest axonal neuropathy.
Slow conduction velocities and prolonged distal latencies imply primary demyelinating neuropathy.
Nonuniform slowing of conduction velocity, conduction block, or temporal dispersion suggests acquired demyelinating neuropathies like GBS or CIDP.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · PATTERN RECOGNITION APPROACH TO NEUROPATHIC DISORDERS | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES

items.title: Laboratory Evaluation in Generalized Symmetric Peripheral Neuropathy
items.content: A comprehensive laboratory evaluation is essential for patients with generalized symmetric peripheral neuropathy to identify underlying causes and guide treatment.
keyPoints: Include complete blood count, basic chemistries (serum electrolytes), renal/hepatic function tests, fasting blood glucose (FBS), hemoglobin A1c, thyroid function tests, B12, folate, ESR, rheumatoid factor, ANA, SPEP and immunoelectrophoresis or immunofixation.
Quantify serum-free light chains and kappa/lambda ratio for sensitivity in detecting monoclonal gammopathy, especially if amyloidosis is suspected.
Perform skeletal survey in patients with acquired demyelinating neuropathies and M-spikes to check for osteosclerotic or lytic lesions.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · OTHER IMPORTANT LABORATORY INFORMATION

items.title: Screening and Workup for Specific Neuropathy Patterns
items.content: Tailored laboratory tests are crucial based on the pattern of neuropathy, such as mononeuropathy multiplex or suspected GBS/CIDP.
keyPoints: For mononeuropathy multiplex, conduct vasculitis workup including ANCAs, cryoglobulins, hepatitis serology, Western blot for Lyme disease, HIV, and CMV titer if indicated.
In suspected GBS or CIDP, perform lumbar puncture to check for elevated CSF protein; absence of pleocytosis suggests idiopathic cases.
Screen for serum IgG4 antibodies in CIDP with severe sensory ataxia, tremor, and distal weakness as they may respond to rituximab.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · OTHER IMPORTANT LABORATORY INFORMATION

items.title: Considerations for Severe Sensory Ataxia and Paraneoplastic Neuropathy
items.content: Evaluate patients with severe sensory ataxia for potential underlying conditions such as Sjögren's syndrome or paraneoplastic neuropathy.
keyPoints: Test for antibodies to SS-A/Ro and SS-B/La in cases of sensory ataxia, especially if dry eyes/mouth are present.
Obtain antineuronal nuclear antibodies (e.g., anti-Hu) to evaluate possible paraneoplastic sensory ganglionopathy, often associated with small-cell carcinoma.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · OTHER IMPORTANT LABORATORY INFORMATION

items.subtopic: NERVE BIOPSIES | SKIN BIOPSIES | HEREDITARY NEUROPATHIES
items.title: Indications and Utility of Nerve Biopsies in Peripheral Neuropathy Evaluation
items.content: Nerve biopsies are rarely indicated for evaluating peripheral neuropathies, primarily reserved for suspected amyloid neuropathy or vasculitis. They should only be performed when nerve conduction studies (NCS) show abnormalities. The sural nerve is typically chosen due to its sensory nature and lack of associated motor function loss.
keyPoints: Indications: Amyloid neuropathy, vasculitis
Performed if NCS are abnormal
Sural nerve commonly biopsied for being a pure sensory nerve
Combination biopsy (superficial peroneal nerve and peroneus brevis muscle) increases diagnostic yield in suspected vasculitis
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · NERVE BIOPSIES | SKIN BIOPSIES | HEREDITARY NEUROPATHIES

items.subtopic: NERVE BIOPSIES | SKIN BIOPSIES | HEREDITARY NEUROPATHIES
items.title: Characteristics and Diagnostic Criteria of Hereditary Neuropathies (CMT)
items.content: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease is the most common hereditary neuropathy, classified by nerve conduction velocities and pathology. Type 1 CMT involves demyelinating sensorimotor neuropathies with motor NCVs <38 m/s, typically between 20-25 m/s. Type 2 CMT features axonal sensory neuropathies with motor NCVs >38 m/s. Both types usually present in childhood or early adulthood and are mostly autosomal dominant.
keyPoints: CMT is the most common hereditary neuropathy
Type 1 CMT: Demyelinating, motor NCV <38 m/s (usually 20-25 m/s)
Type 2 CMT: Axonal sensory, motor NCV >38 m/s
Onset typically in childhood or early adulthood
Inheritance is mostly autosomal dominant
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · NERVE BIOPSIES | SKIN BIOPSIES | HEREDITARY NEUROPATHIES

items.title: Diagnosis of CMT1: Non-invasive Testing Preferred
items.content: CMT1, the most common hereditary neuropathy, is typically diagnosed using non-invasive methods such as nerve conduction studies (NCS) and genetic testing. Nerve biopsies are generally not performed due to their invasive nature and because diagnosis can be effectively achieved with less invasive tests.
keyPoints: CMT1 is the most common hereditary neuropathy.
Diagnosis usually made by NCS and genetic studies, avoiding nerve biopsy.
Nerve biopsies show reduced myelinated fibers and onion bulb formations.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CMT1

items.title: Genetic Basis of CMT1A: Duplication on Chromosome 17p11.2-12
items.content: CMT1A, the most prevalent subtype of CMT1, is caused by a duplication within chromosome 17p11.2-12 leading to an extra copy of the PMP-22 gene. This genetic anomaly results in three copies of the gene instead of two, affecting myelin protein composition.
keyPoints: CMT1A represents 70% of CMT1 cases.
Caused by a 1.5-megabase duplication on chromosome 17p11.2-12.
Results in three copies of the PMP-22 gene, accounting for 2–5% of myelin protein.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CMT1

items.title: Distinguishing CMT2 from CMT1
items.content: CMT2 is less frequent than CMT1 and presents later in life. Clinically similar to CMT1, it can be differentiated by nerve conduction studies showing normal or slightly reduced velocities.
keyPoints: CMT2 occurs approximately half as frequently as CMT1
Symptomatic onset usually in the second decade; some cases present earlier or later
Clinically indistinguishable from CMT1, but NCS show normal or slightly reduced velocities
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CMT2 (part 1) | CMT2 (part 2)

items.title: Presentation Timing of CMT2
items.content: CMT2 typically presents in the second decade, but can vary with some cases appearing earlier in childhood or remaining asymptomatic into late adult life.
keyPoints: Symptomatic onset usually in the second decade
Some cases present earlier in childhood
Others remain asymptomatic into late adult life
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CMT2 (part 1) | CMT2 (part 2)

items.title: Role of Nerve Conduction Studies in Diagnosing CMT2
items.content: Nerve conduction studies are crucial for differentiating CMT2 from CMT1, as they show normal or slightly reduced velocities in CMT2.
keyPoints: NCS help differentiate CMT2 from CMT1
CMT2 shows normal or slightly reduced nerve conduction velocities
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CMT2 (part 1) | CMT2 (part 2)

items.title: Understanding the Genetic Basis of CMT2
items.content: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 2 (CMT2) is primarily caused by mutations in the MFN2 gene, which plays a crucial role in mitochondrial fusion and network architecture. This mutation accounts for approximately 20-30% of all CMT2 cases.
keyPoints: MFN2 mutation accounts for ~20–30% of CMT2 cases.
MFN2 is located on the outer mitochondrial membrane.
Regulates mitochondrial fusion.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CMT2 (part 3) | CMT3 | CMT4

items.title: Characteristics and Genetic Causes of CMT3
items.content: Originally described as a hereditary demyelinating sensorimotor polyneuropathy, CMT3 presents in infancy or early childhood with severe weakness. It is characterized by markedly slowed motor NCVs (≤5–10 m/s) and is caused by point mutations in genes also responsible for CMT1.
keyPoints: CMT3 presents in infancy/early childhood.
Severe weakness observed.
Motor NCVs ≤5–10 m/s.
Caused by mutations in PMP-22, MPZ, or ERG-2 genes.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CMT2 (part 3) | CMT3 | CMT4

items.title: Understanding CMTX1: Severe Neuropathy in Males and Genetic Basis
items.content: CMTX1, the most common form of X-linked Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (CMT), presents with severe neuropathy predominantly in males. It accounts for approximately 10-15% of all CMT cases. Clinical features include atrophy and weakness of distal limbs, areflexia, pes cavus, and hammer toes. Males typically present symptoms early, while females may remain asymptomatic or develop milder symptoms later.
keyPoints: CMTX1 accounts for ~10–15% of CMT overall.
Males exhibit more severe neuropathy than females.
Motor NCVs in males are moderately slowed (low to mid 30-m/s range).
~50% of males have motor NCVs between 15 and 35 m/s, with ~80% falling between 25 and 35 m/s.
CMTX1 is caused by mutations in GJB1 encoding connexin-32.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CMTX

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for CMTX1: Nerve Conduction Studies (NCS)
items.content: Nerve conduction studies are crucial in diagnosing CMTX1, revealing both demyelination and axonal degeneration. In males with CMTX1, motor nerve conduction velocities (NCVs) are significantly slowed, while females often have normal or intermediate NCVs.
keyPoints: NCS show features of demyelination and axonal degeneration in CMTX1.
Males: Motor NCVs in the low to mid 30-m/s range; ~50% between 15-35 m/s, with ~80% between 25-35 m/s.
Females: ~80% have normal NCVs; 20% have intermediate NCVs.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CMTX

items.title: Genetic Basis of CMTX1: Role of Connexin-32 Mutations
items.content: CMTX1 is caused by mutations in the GJB1 gene, which encodes connexin-32. This protein plays a critical role in cell-to-cell communication through gap junctions, and its dysfunction leads to the clinical manifestations seen in CMTX1.
keyPoints: CMTX1 results from mutations in GJB1 encoding connexin-32.
Connexins are essential for cell-to-cell communication.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CMTX

items.subtopic: SORBITAL DEHYDROGENASE DEFICIENCY WITH PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY | FABRY'S DISEASE
items.title: Understanding SORD and its Presentation as a Common Autosomal Recessive Neuropathy
items.content: Sorbitol dehydrogenase deficiency (SORD) is recognized as the most common autosomal recessive inherited neuropathy. It manifests as a slowly progressive, length-dependent axonal motor greater than sensory polyneuropathy or pure motor neuropathy, typically beginning in late teens.
keyPoints: Most common autosomal recessive inherited form of neuropathy
Age of onset usually in the late teens
Caused by pathogenic mutations in the SORD gene
SORD is part of the polyol pathway converting glucose to fructose
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · SORBITAL DEHYDROGENASE DEFICIENCY WITH PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY | FABRY'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: SORBITAL DEHYDROGENASE DEFICIENCY WITH PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY | FABRY'S DISEASE
items.title: Fabry's Disease: Clinical Features and Management Strategies
items.content: Fabry's disease is an X-linked dominant disorder characterized by angiokeratomas, neuropathic pain, and severe systemic complications. Early treatment with enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) may improve outcomes.
keyPoints: X-linked dominant disorder affecting more severely men than women
Angiokeratomas typically found around the umbilicus, scrotum, inguinal region, and perineum
Neuropathy often overshadowed by premature atherosclerosis complications
ERT options: agalsidase-α (Replagal; 0.2 mg/kg body weight), agalsidase-β (Fabrazyme; 1 mg/kg body weight), pegunigalsidase (Elfabrio) every 2 weeks
Migalastat is an oral pharmacologic chaperone for amenable mutations (~50% of patients)
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · SORBITAL DEHYDROGENASE DEFICIENCY WITH PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY | FABRY'S DISEASE

items.title: Recognizing Adrenomyeloneuropathy (AMN) and its Management
items.content: Adrenomyeloneuropathy, an allelic X-linked dominant disorder related to ALD, typically presents in the third to fifth decade with mild to moderate peripheral neuropathy and progressive spastic paraplegia. Diagnosis is supported by elevated very-long-chain fatty acid (VLCFA) levels (C24, C25, and C26) in urine and adrenal insufficiency in about two-thirds of patients.
keyPoints: AMN presents with peripheral neuropathy and progressive spastic paraplegia typically between the third to fifth decade.
Approximately 30% of patients with mutations in the ABC transporter gene present with AMN phenotype.
VLCFA levels (C24, C25, and C26) are increased in urine.
Adrenal insufficiency is evident in about two-thirds of patients.
Diagnosis confirmed by genetic testing; no proven effective therapy for neurologic manifestations.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · ADRENOLEUKODYSTROPHY/ADRENOMYELONEUROPATHY | REFSUM'S DISEASE

items.title: Understanding Refsum's Disease and its Treatment
items.content: Refsum's disease is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by a tetrad of peripheral neuropathy, retinitis pigmentosa, cerebellar ataxia, and elevated CSF protein concentration. Elevated serum phytanic acid levels are diagnostic, with treatment focusing on dietary management to remove phytanic precursors.
keyPoints: Classic tetrad includes peripheral neuropathy, retinitis pigmentosa, cerebellar ataxia, and elevated CSF protein.
Progressive distal sensory loss and weakness in legs leading to foot drop by twenties.
Elevated serum phytanic acid levels are diagnostic.
Treatment involves dietary removal of phytanic precursors (e.g., fish oils, dairy products, ruminant fats).
Genetic mutations include those affecting PAHX and PRX7 genes.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · ADRENOLEUKODYSTROPHY/ADRENOMYELONEUROPATHY | REFSUM'S DISEASE

items.title: Recognize Tangier Disease through Neuropathic Presentations and Lipid Abnormalities
items.content: Tangier disease, an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the ABC1 gene, presents with neuropathy patterns including asymmetric mononeuropathies, symmetric polyneuropathy predominantly in legs, or pseudo-syringomyelia. It is characterized by low HDL cholesterol and increased triacylglycerol levels.
keyPoints: Tangier disease can present as (1) asymmetric multiple mononeuropathies, (2) a slowly progressive symmetric polyneuropathy predominantly in the legs, or (3) a pseudo-syringomyelia pattern with dissociated sensory loss.
Swollen and yellowish-orange tonsils, splenomegaly, and lymphadenopathy may be present.
Caused by mutations in the ATP-binding cassette transporter 1 (ABC1) gene.
Results in markedly reduced levels of HDL cholesterol and increased triacylglycerol levels.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · TANGIER DISEASE | PORPHYRIA

items.title: Identify Porphyria with Acute Neurologic Symptoms and Neuropathic Patterns
items.content: Porphyrias, particularly AIP, HCP, and VP, can present with acute neurologic symptoms such as abdominal pain, agitation, hallucinations, seizures, followed by back/extremity pain and weakness. These mimic GBS and are associated with specific urinary porphyrin metabolites.
keyPoints: Acute attacks may begin with sharp abdominal pain, followed by neurological symptoms like agitation, hallucinations, or seizures.
Weakness can be asymmetric, proximal, or distal, affecting arms, legs, face, and bulbar musculature.
CSF protein is typically normal or mildly elevated; liver function tests and hematologic parameters are usually normal.
Urinary porphyrin metabolites include d-aminolevulinic acid, porphobilinogen, uroporphobilinogen, coproporphyrinogen, and protoporphyrinogen.
Treatment includes intravenous glucose (10–20 g/h) and hematin (2–5 mg/kg per day for 3–14 days if no improvement).
Givosiran reduces attack frequency in acute intermittent porphyria.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · TANGIER DISEASE | PORPHYRIA

items.subtopic: CEREBELLAR ATAXIA, NEUROPATHY, AND VESTIBULAR AREFLEXIA SYNDROME (CANVAS)
items.title: Recognizing CANVAS: The Common Autosomal Recessive Ataxia
items.content: CANVAS is the most common cause of autosomal recessive ataxia, typically manifesting in middle adulthood. It progresses over 10–15 years to include sensory neuropathy/neuronopathy, cerebellar and vestibular dysfunction, and a dry cough. Clinical presentation can vary widely, with some patients showing upper and lower motor neuron involvement similar to amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
keyPoints: Most common cause of autosomal recessive ataxia.
Manifests in middle adult life; progresses over 10–15 years.
Symptoms include sensory neuropathy/neuronopathy, cerebellar and vestibular dysfunction, dry cough.
Clinical spectrum includes upper and lower motor neuron involvement (spasticity, brisk reflexes, muscle atrophy, fasciculations).
Associated with biallelic (AAGGG) _{n} repeat expansions in RFC1 gene.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CEREBELLAR ATAXIA, NEUROPATHY, AND VESTIBULAR AREFLEXIA SYNDROME (CANVAS) | FAMILIAL AMYLOID POLYNEUROPATHY

items.title: Diagnosing and Managing TTR-related Familial Amyloid Polyneuropathy (FAP)
items.content: TTR-related FAP typically presents in the third to fourth decade with numbness and painful paresthesias in distal lower limbs. Carpal tunnel syndrome is common, and autonomic involvement can be severe. Patients often die 10–15 years after symptom onset due to cardiac failure or malnutrition complications. Liver transplantation and medications like tafamidis meglumine and diflunisal can slow disease progression.
keyPoints: TTR-related FAP presents with numbness, painful paresthesias in distal lower limbs (third to fourth decade).
Carpal tunnel syndrome is common; severe autonomic involvement possible.
Patients typically die 10–15 years after onset from cardiac failure or malnutrition complications.
Liver transplantation and medications like tafamidis meglumine, diflunisal can slow progression.
Gene therapies (e.g., patisiran, vutrisiran) are effective in reducing TTR synthesis.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CEREBELLAR ATAXIA, NEUROPATHY, AND VESTIBULAR AREFLEXIA SYNDROME (CANVAS) | FAMILIAL AMYLOID POLYNEUROPATHY

items.title: Understanding Gelsolin-related Amyloidosis: Finnish Type
items.content: Gelsolin-related amyloidosis, or Finnish type, begins in the third decade with lattice corneal dystrophy and multiple cranial neuropathies. Over time, a mild generalized sensorimotor polyneuropathy develops, but autonomic dysfunction is not typically present.
keyPoints: Characterized by lattice corneal dystrophy and multiple cranial neuropathies starting in the third decade.
Progresses to mild generalized sensorimotor polyneuropathy.
Autonomic dysfunction does not occur.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CEREBELLAR ATAXIA, NEUROPATHY, AND VESTIBULAR AREFLEXIA SYNDROME (CANVAS) | FAMILIAL AMYLOID POLYNEUROPATHY

items.title: Clinical Presentation of AL Amyloidosis Neuropathy
items.content: AL amyloidosis often presents with a polyneuropathy characterized by painful dysesthesias and burning sensations, primarily in the feet but can involve the trunk or present as mononeuropathy multiplex. Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) may be an initial manifestation in 25% of patients.
keyPoints: Approximately 30% of AL amyloidosis patients present with polyneuropathy.
Painful dysesthesias and burning sensations are common, especially in the feet.
CTS occurs in 25% of patients and may be the initial manifestation.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · PRIMARY OR AL AMYLOIDOSIS (SEE CHAP. 117)

items.title: Prognosis and Treatment Considerations in AL Amyloidosis
items.content: The median survival for patients with primary amyloidosis is less than 2 years, often due to progressive congestive heart failure or renal failure. Chemotherapy with melphalan, prednisone, and colchicine may prolong survival, but the improvement of neuropathy remains controversial.
keyPoints: Median survival <2 years, typically from cardiac or renal complications.
Chemotherapy options include melphalan, prednisone, and colchicine.
Neuropathy improvement with treatment is controversial.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · PRIMARY OR AL AMYLOIDOSIS (SEE CHAP. 117)

items.title: Diabetic Distal Symmetric Sensory and Sensorimotor Polyneuropathy (DSPN) Presentation and Management
items.content: DSPN is the most prevalent form of diabetic neuropathy, characterized by sensory loss starting in the toes and progressing upwards. It may present with tingling, burning, or deep aching pains. Nerve conduction studies typically show reduced amplitudes and mild to moderate slowing of conduction velocities. Tight glucose control can reduce the risk or improve existing neuropathy. Treatment for painful symptoms includes anticonvulsants, antidepressants, sodium channel blockers, and other analgesics.
keyPoints: Most common form of diabetic neuropathy
Sensory loss begins in toes, progresses up legs and into arms
Nerve conduction studies show reduced amplitudes and mild to moderate slowing
Tight glucose control can reduce risk or improve neuropathy
Medications for pain: anticonvulsants, antidepressants, sodium channel blockers
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · DIABETIC NEUROPATHY

items.title: Diabetic Autonomic Neuropathy Manifestations and Diagnostic Testing
items.content: Autonomic neuropathy often accompanies DSPN in diabetic patients. It can manifest with symptoms such as abnormal sweating, dysfunctional thermoregulation, dry eyes/mouth, pupillary abnormalities, cardiac arrhythmias, postural hypotension, GI issues (e.g., gastroparesis), and genitourinary dysfunction. Diagnostic tests like sympathetic skin responses and quantitative sudomotor axon reflex testing are generally abnormal.
keyPoints: Commonly seen with DSPN
Symptoms: abnormal sweating, dysfunctional thermoregulation, dry eyes/mouth, pupillary abnormalities, cardiac arrhythmias, postural hypotension, GI issues, genitourinary dysfunction
Diagnostic tests: sympathetic skin responses and quantitative sudomotor axon reflex testing are abnormal
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · DIABETIC NEUROPATHY

items.title: Diabetic Radiculoplexus Neuropathy (Bruns-Garland Syndrome) Characteristics and Management
items.content: This neuropathy often presents with severe pain in the low back, hip, and thigh, usually affecting one leg. It can lead to muscle atrophy and weakness in both proximal and distal muscles of the affected leg. The condition is frequently accompanied by significant weight loss. CSF protein levels are typically elevated, while cell count remains normal, and ESR may be increased. Electromyography shows active denervation. In severe cases with acute pain, glucocorticoids might be used, although no randomized controlled trial has confirmed their efficacy.
keyPoints: Presents with severe pain in low back, hip, thigh
Affects one leg initially; can progress to both legs
Muscle atrophy and weakness in affected leg
Often accompanied by significant weight loss
CSF protein elevated, cell count normal, ESR often increased
EDx shows active denervation
Glucocorticoids used for severe pain (lack of RCT evidence)
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · DIABETIC NEUROPATHY

items.title: Neuropathy in Hypothyroidism: Recognize and Treat Underlying Cause
items.content: While hypothyroidism is more commonly associated with proximal myopathy, it can also lead to neuropathies such as carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) or a generalized sensory polyneuropathy. The latter presents with painful paresthesias and numbness in both legs and hands.
keyPoints: Hypothyroidism can cause CTS or generalized sensory polyneuropathy characterized by painful paresthesias and numbness.
Treatment involves correcting the underlying hypothyroidism.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · HYPOTHYROIDISM | SJÖGREN'S SYNDROME | RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS

items.title: Neuropathy in Sjögren's Syndrome: Identify Patterns and Manage Accordingly
items.content: Sjögren's syndrome can lead to various neuropathies, including length-dependent axonal sensorimotor neuropathy with sensory loss in distal extremities. It may also present as a pure small-fiber neuropathy or cranial neuropathy involving the trigeminal nerve. Sensory neuronopathy/ganglionopathy is characterized by non-length-dependent numbness and tingling affecting limbs, trunk, and face.
keyPoints: Sjögren's syndrome can cause length-dependent axonal sensorimotor neuropathy with sensory loss in distal extremities.
Neuropathies may include small-fiber neuropathy or cranial involvement (e.g., trigeminal nerve).
Sensory neuronopathy/ganglionopathy presents with non-length-dependent numbness and tingling, severe vibratory and proprioceptive loss leading to sensory ataxia.
Immunotherapy such as intravenous immunoglobulin may stabilize or improve symptoms in some cases.

items.title: Peripheral Neuropathy in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: Vasculitis and Treatment Implications
items.content: In SLE, peripheral neuropathy occurs in 2-27% of patients, often presenting as a slowly progressive sensory loss starting in the feet. Small-fiber neuropathy is common, while vasculitic mononeuropathies are less frequent but may benefit from immunosuppressive therapy.
keyPoints: Peripheral neuropathy prevalence: 2-27% in SLE
Common presentation: slowly progressive sensory loss starting in feet
Small-fiber neuropathy is common (Pattern 2, Table 457-2)
Vasculitic mononeuropathies may benefit from immunosuppressive therapy

items.title: Neuropathic Manifestations in Systemic Sclerosis: Patterns and Characteristics
items.content: Systemic sclerosis often involves a distal symmetric sensory polyneuropathy (5-67%), with cranial mononeuropathies, particularly of the trigeminal nerve. Axonal sensory greater than motor polyneuropathy is characteristic.
keyPoints: Peripheral neuropathy prevalence: 5-67% in systemic sclerosis
Common presentation: distal symmetric sensory polyneuropathy (Pattern 2, Table 457-2)
Cranial mononeuropathies, especially trigeminal nerve involvement
Axonal sensory greater than motor polyneuropathy on EDx and biopsy

items.title: Peripheral Neuropathy in Hyper-eosinophilic Syndrome and Celiac Disease: Prevalence and Pathogenesis
items.content: Hyper-eosinophilic syndrome can lead to peripheral neuropathy or mononeuropathy multiplex in 6–14% of patients. In celiac disease, neurologic complications like ataxia and peripheral neuropathy occur in about 10% of cases. The neuropathy may be due to malabsorption of vitamins B12 and E, though some patients show no vitamin deficiencies, suggesting an autoimmune etiology.
keyPoints: Peripheral neuropathy or mononeuropathy multiplex occurs in 6–14% of hyper-eosinophilic syndrome patients.
Neurologic complications occur in about 10% of celiac disease patients.
Celiac-related neuropathy may be due to malabsorption of vitamins B12 and E, but some cases show no vitamin deficiencies.
The pathogenic basis for celiac neuropathy might be autoimmune.

items.title: Management of Peripheral Neuropathy in Celiac Disease and Uremic Conditions
items.content: In celiac disease, neuropathy does not typically respond to a gluten-free diet. However, vitamin B12 or E replacement can improve or stabilize the condition if deficiencies are present. In uremic patients, polyneuropathy affects about 60% of those with renal failure and is characterized by length-dependent numbness and tingling. Hemodialysis can stabilize this neuropathy, while successful renal transplantation may lead to improvement.
keyPoints: Celiac disease-related neuropathy does not respond to a gluten-free diet.
Vitamin B12 or E replacement therapy may improve celiac-related neuropathy if deficiencies are present.
Approximately 60% of patients with renal failure develop polyneuropathy characterized by length-dependent symptoms.
Hemodialysis can stabilize uremic polyneuropathy, and successful renal transplantation may lead to improvement.

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LIVER DISEASE | CRITICAL ILLNESS POLYNEUROPATHY
items.title: Peripheral Neuropathy in Chronic Liver Disease: Consider Underlying Causes
items.content: In patients with chronic liver failure, a generalized sensorimotor neuropathy often presents as numbness, tingling, and minor weakness primarily affecting the lower limbs. Electrophysiological studies typically show sensory axonopathy greater than motor involvement. Sural nerve biopsy may reveal segmental demyelination and axonal loss. It remains uncertain if hepatic failure alone can cause peripheral neuropathy since most patients have liver disease secondary to conditions like alcoholism or viral hepatitis, which are also known to induce neuropathy.
keyPoints: Generalized sensorimotor neuropathy is common in chronic liver failure.
Electrophysiological studies show sensory axonopathy greater than motor involvement.
Sural nerve biopsy reveals segmental demyelination and axonal loss.
Hepatic failure alone as a cause of peripheral neuropathy is uncertain; often associated with alcoholism or viral hepatitis.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CHRONIC LIVER DISEASE | CRITICAL ILLNESS POLYNEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LIVER DISEASE | CRITICAL ILLNESS POLYNEUROPATHY
items.title: Distinguishing Critical Illness Polyneuropathy (CIP) from Myopathy in ICU Patients
items.content: Critical illness polyneuropathy (CIP) and critical illness myopathy (CIM) are common causes of acute generalized weakness in critically ill patients, often complicating weaning from ventilators. Distinguishing between CIP and CIM can be challenging clinically and electrophysiologically. Serum creatine kinase (CK) levels are typically normal in CIP but elevated in CIM. Nerve conduction studies show reduced amplitudes for both motor and sensory functions in CIP, whereas sensory function is relatively preserved in CIM. Needle electromyography often reveals positive sharp waves and fibrillation potentials.
keyPoints: CIP and CIM are common causes of acute weakness in ICU patients.
Serum CK levels: normal in CIP, elevated in CIM.
Nerve conduction studies show reduced amplitudes for both motor and sensory functions in CIP; sensory preserved in CIM.
Needle EMG reveals positive sharp waves and fibrillation potentials.

items.title: Diagnosis of Leprosy
items.content: Leprosy is diagnosed primarily through skin lesion biopsy, but nerve biopsy can also be diagnostic, especially when no apparent skin lesions are present. The tuberculoid form shows granulomas without visible bacilli, while lepromatous leprosy reveals numerous infiltrating bacilli and foamy macrophages with minimal granulomatous infiltration.
keyPoints: Leprosy is caused by Mycobacterium leprae.
Skin lesion biopsy is the usual diagnostic method.
Nerve biopsy can be diagnostic in absence of skin lesions.
Tuberculoid form: characterized by granulomas, no visible bacilli.
Lepromatous leprosy: large numbers of infiltrating bacilli, TH2 lymphocytes, foamy macrophages with minimal granulomatous infiltration.
Bacilli best appreciated using Fite stain as red-staining rods.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · LEPROSY (HANSEN'S DISEASE)

items.title: Management of Leprosy and its Complications
items.content: Leprosy treatment typically involves a multi-drug regimen including dapsone, rifampin, and clofazimine for 2 years. The reversal reaction, common in borderline leprosy, is managed with high-dose glucocorticoids to blunt the adverse effects of increased cellular immunity.
keyPoints: Treatment involves multiple drugs: dapsone, rifampin, clofazimine.
Additional medications include thalidomide, pefloxacin, ofloxacin, sparfloxacin, minocycline, clarithromycin.
Standard treatment duration is 2 years.
Reversal reaction occurs due to a shift towards tuberculoid leprosy during treatment.
High-dose glucocorticoids are used to manage reversal reactions and may be prophylactic in high-risk patients.
Erythema nodosum leprosum (ENL) is treated with glucocorticoids or thalidomide.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · LEPROSY (HANSEN'S DISEASE)

items.title: Neurologic Complications of Lyme Disease
items.content: Lyme disease, caused by Borrelia burgdorferi, can lead to neurologic complications during its second and third stages. Facial neuropathy is common and often bilateral, distinguishing it from idiopathic Bell's palsy. Patients may also present with polyradiculoneuropathy or multiple mononeuropathies.
keyPoints: Facial neuropathy in Lyme disease is bilateral in about half of cases.
Neurologic involvement is frequently asymmetric.
EDx suggests a primary axonopathy.
Nerve biopsies may show axonal degeneration with perivascular inflammation.
Treatment involves antibiotics.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · LYME DISEASE | DIPHTHERITIC NEUROPATHY | COVID-19

items.title: Diphtheritic Neuropathy and Its Management
items.content: Diphtheria, caused by Corynebacterium diphtheriae, can lead to peripheral neuropathy in 20-70% of patients due to bacterial toxin. Symptoms include decreased throat sensation, dysphagia, dysarthria, hoarseness, blurred vision, and a generalized polyneuropathy characterized by numbness, paresthesias, and weakness.
keyPoints: Peripheral neuropathy occurs in 20-70% of diphtheritic patients.
Symptoms can develop 3-4 weeks post-infection.
CSF protein may be elevated with or without lymphocytic pleocytosis.
EDx suggests a diffuse axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy.
Antitoxin and antibiotics should be administered within 48 hours of symptom onset.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · LYME DISEASE | DIPHTHERITIC NEUROPATHY | COVID-19

items.title: Understanding HIV-Related Distal Symmetric Polyneuropathy (DSP)
items.content: HIV-related DSP is the most common peripheral neuropathy in AIDS patients, characterized by numbness and painful paresthesias in distal extremities. It's not due to direct infection of nerves but may be immune-mediated or related to neurotoxicity from older antiretroviral agents.
keyPoints: Approximately 20% of HIV-infected individuals develop neuropathy.
DSP is characterized by numbness and painful paresthesias in distal extremities (Pattern 2, Table 457-2).
Not due to direct infection of peripheral nerves; may be immune-mediated or neurotoxic from older antiretrovirals.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · HUMAN IMMUNODEFICIENCY VIRUS

items.title: Diagnosing HIV-Related Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyradiculoneuropathy
items.content: AIDP and CIDP can occur in HIV patients, with AIDP often at seroconversion and CIDP anytime during infection. CSF shows elevated protein levels and lymphocytic pleocytosis, distinguishing it from idiopathic forms.
keyPoints: Both AIDP and CIDP can be complications of HIV (Pattern 1, Table 457-2).
AIDP typically occurs at seroconversion; CIDP can occur anytime during infection.
CSF shows elevated protein levels and lymphocytic pleocytosis.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · HUMAN IMMUNODEFICIENCY VIRUS

items.title: Understanding Herpes Zoster Reactivation and Postherpetic Neuralgia
items.content: Herpes varicella-zoster virus (HVZ) can reactivate, causing peripheral neuropathy characterized by severe pain and paresthesias in a dermatomal region followed by a vesicular rash. Weakness may occur in muscles innervated by roots corresponding to the affected skin lesions in 5–30% of patients. Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) affects approximately 25% of patients, with vaccination reducing HVZ incidence by 51% and PHN by 67%. Treatment for PHN is symptomatic.
keyPoints: Two-thirds of adult infections present with dermal zoster.
Weakness in innervated muscles occurs in 5–30% of cases.
Approximately 25% develop postherpetic neuralgia (PHN).
Vaccination reduces HVZ incidence by 51% and PHN by 67%.
Treatment for PHN is symptomatic.

items.title: Recognizing Paraneoplastic Sensory Neuronopathy in Cancer Patients
items.content: Paraneoplastic encephalomyelitis/sensory neuronopathy (PEM/SN) often complicates small-cell lung carcinoma, presenting with numbness and paresthesias that are asymmetric. It leads to sensory ataxia due to proprioception loss. Weakness may occur from associated myelitis or motor neuronopathy. Patients might also experience confusion, memory loss, depression, hallucinations, seizures, or cerebellar ataxia. The majority of patients have polyclonal antineuronal antibodies (IgG) against a 35- to 40-kDa protein complex known as Hu antigen. CSF may show mild lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein. Treatment of the underlying cancer does not typically alter PEM/SN progression, though some improvement can occur post-tumor treatment.
keyPoints: PEM/SN commonly complicates small-cell lung carcinoma.
Symptoms include asymmetric numbness, paresthesias, and sensory ataxia.
Weakness may be secondary to myelitis or motor neuronopathy.
Polyclonal antineuronal antibodies (IgG) against Hu antigen are common.
CSF may show mild lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein.
Treatment of the underlying cancer generally does not affect PEM/SN progression.

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHY SECONDARY TO TUMOR INFILTRATION | NEUROPATHY AS A COMPLICATION OF BONE MARROW TRANSPLANTATION | LYMPHOMA
items.title: Neuropathy as a Presentation or Complication of Malignancy and Bone Marrow Transplantation
items.content: Neuropathies can arise from tumor infiltration by malignant cells such as leukemia and lymphoma, presenting as various patterns including mononeuropathy multiplex and polyneuropathy. These neuropathies may be painful and serve as initial symptoms of cancer or indicate relapse. In the context of bone marrow transplantation (BMT), neuropathies can result from chemotherapy toxicity, radiation, infection, autoimmune responses, or graft-versus-host disease (GVHD). Chronic GVHD might lead to immune-mediated peripheral nerve damage.
keyPoints: Neuropathy patterns include mononeuropathy multiplex and polyneuropathy; Patterns 1, 2, 3, and 4 are noted in tumor infiltration.
Neuropathies may be painful and can indicate cancer presentation or relapse.
In BMT, neuropathies may stem from chemotherapy toxicity, radiation, infection, autoimmune responses, or GVHD.
Chronic GVHD might cause immune-mediated peripheral nerve damage; Patterns 1, 2, and 3 are noted in BMT-related neuropathy.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · NEUROPATHY SECONDARY TO TUMOR INFILTRATION | NEUROPATHY AS A COMPLICATION OF BONE MARROW TRANSPLANTATION | LYMPHOMA

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHY SECONDARY TO TUMOR INFILTRATION | NEUROPATHY AS A COMPLICATION OF BONE MARROW TRANSPLANTATION | LYMPHOMA
items.title: Diagnostic and Therapeutic Approaches to Neuropathy in Lymphoma and Post-BMT Patients
items.content: Lymphomas can cause neuropathies through nerve infiltration, compression, or paraneoplastic processes. These neuropathies may present as sensorimotor, symmetric, asymmetric, or multifocal patterns with acute, progressive, or relapsing courses. Diagnosis might involve electrodiagnostics (EDx) showing axonal or demyelinating features and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis revealing lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein levels. Nerve biopsy can show endoneurial inflammatory cells and a monoclonal cell population indicative of lymphomatous invasion.
keyPoints: Lymphoma-related neuropathies may present as sensorimotor, symmetric, asymmetric, or multifocal patterns; Patterns 1, 2, and 3 are noted.
EDx can indicate axonal or demyelinating processes in lymphoma-associated neuropathy.
CSF analysis might show lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein levels.
Nerve biopsy may reveal endoneurial inflammatory cells; a monoclonal cell population suggests lymphomatous invasion.

items.title: Neuropathy Patterns and Diagnostic Approach in Multiple Myeloma
items.content: Multiple myeloma often presents with neuropathy in up to 40% of patients, typically as a distal axonal sensory or sensorimotor polyneuropathy. Less commonly, it may present as chronic demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy. Diagnostic evaluation includes identifying monoclonal proteins and performing EDx studies showing reduced amplitudes with normal or mildly abnormal latencies and conduction velocities.
keyPoints: Neuropathy occurs in up to 40% of MM patients
Common neuropathy pattern: distal axonal sensory/sensorimotor polyneuropathy (Pattern 2)
Less common: chronic demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (Pattern 1)
Monoclonal proteins usually composed of gamma or mu heavy chains, or kappa light chains
EDx shows reduced amplitudes with normal/mildly abnormal latencies and conduction velocities

items.title: Risk Factors and Mechanisms of Chemotherapy-Induced Neuropathies
items.content: Chemotherapy agents can cause toxic neuropathies, with mechanisms varying by agent. Patients with preexisting neuropathy or concurrent use of other neurotoxic drugs are at higher risk. These neuropathies often present as sensory greater than motor length-dependent axonal neuropathy.
keyPoints: Chemotherapy agents can induce toxic neuropathies
Higher risk in patients with preexisting neuropathy (e.g., CMT, diabetic neuropathy)
Increased risk with concurrent use of neurotoxic drugs (e.g., nitrofurantoin, isoniazid)
Typically presents as sensory greater than motor length-dependent axonal neuropathy

items.title: Toxic Myopathy and Neuropathy from Chloroquine and Hydroxychloroquine: Mechanisms and Management
items.content: Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine can lead to toxic myopathy characterized by proximal muscle weakness and atrophy, more pronounced in the legs. This condition may be accompanied by neuropathy presenting as sensory loss and distal weakness. The neuromyopathy typically manifests after prolonged use of 500 mg daily for a year or more but has been reported with doses as low as 200 mg/d. Elevated serum CK levels are common due to myopathy, while NCS may show mild slowing of motor and sensory nerve conduction velocities (NCVs) and reduced amplitudes. EMG findings include myopathic MUAPs and increased insertional activity. The neuropathy's pathogenesis is linked to the drug's amphiphilic properties, leading to autophagic vacuoles in Schwann cells and muscle fibers.
keyPoints: Toxic myopathy characterized by proximal weakness and atrophy, worse in legs than arms.
Neuropathy can develop with or without myopathy, presenting as sensory loss and distal weakness.
Typically appears after prolonged use of 500 mg daily for a year or more; reported with doses as low as 200 mg/d.
Serum CK levels usually elevated due to myopathy.
NCS may show mild slowing of motor and sensory NCVs, mild to moderate reduction in amplitudes.
EMG shows myopathic MUAPs, increased insertional activity (positive sharp waves, fibrillation potentials), occasionally myotonic potentials.
Neuropathy pathogenesis involves drug-lipid complexes resistant to lysosomal digestion, forming autophagic vacuoles.
Symptoms usually reversible after discontinuation of medication.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CHLOROQUINE AND HYDROXYCHLOROQUINE | AMIODARONE

items.title: Amiodarone-Induced Neuromyopathy: Pathogenesis and Persistence
items.content: Amiodarone can cause a neuromyopathy similar to that induced by chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine. This condition typically appears after 2–3 years of medication use. Nerve biopsy reveals segmental demyelination and axonal loss, with electron microscopy showing lamellar or dense inclusions in Schwann cells, pericytes, and endothelial cells. These pathological changes can persist for up to 2 years even after discontinuation of the drug.
keyPoints: Neuromyopathy similar to chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine-induced conditions.
Typically appears after 2–3 years of amiodarone use.
Nerve biopsy shows segmental demyelination and axonal loss.
Electron microscopy reveals lamellar or dense inclusions in Schwann cells, pericytes, endothelial cells.
Pathological changes can persist for up to 2 years post-discontinuation.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · CHLOROQUINE AND HYDROXYCHLOROQUINE | AMIODARONE

items.subtopic: COLCHICINE | THALIDOMIDE | PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B₆) TOXICITY | ISONIAZID
items.title: Colchicine Neuromyopathy: Mechanism and Clinical Features
items.content: Colchicine can cause neuromyopathy characterized by proximal weakness, numbness, and tingling in the distal extremities. Electrophysiological studies reveal axonal polyneuropathy features, while muscle biopsy shows vacuolar myopathy, and sensory nerves exhibit axonal degeneration. The mechanism involves colchicine's inhibition of tubulin polymerization into microtubules, disrupting intracellular protein, nutrient, and waste movement.
keyPoints: Colchicine inhibits the polymerization of tubulin into microtubules.
Clinical presentation includes proximal weakness and distal numbness/tingling.
Electrophysiology shows axonal polyneuropathy features.
Muscle biopsy reveals vacuolar myopathy; sensory nerves show axonal degeneration.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · COLCHICINE | THALIDOMIDE | PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B₆) TOXICITY | ISONIAZID

items.subtopic: COLCHICINE | THALIDOMIDE | PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B₆) TOXICITY | ISONIAZID
items.title: Isoniazid-Induced Peripheral Neuropathy: Risk Factors and Prevention
items.content: Peripheral neuropathy is a common side effect of isoniazid, with a 2% incidence at standard doses (3–5 mg/kg per day) and up to 17% in those taking more than 6 mg/kg per day. High-risk groups include the elderly, malnourished, and slow acetylators due to INH's inhibition of pyridoxal phosphokinase, leading to pyridoxine deficiency. Prophylactic administration of pyridoxine (100 mg/d) can prevent neuropathy.
keyPoints: Standard dose INH (3–5 mg/kg per day) has a 2% incidence of neuropathy.
Neuropathy occurs in at least 17% with doses >6 mg/kg per day.
High-risk groups: elderly, malnourished, slow acetylators.
INH inhibits pyridoxal phosphokinase, causing pyridoxine deficiency.
Prophylactic pyridoxine (100 mg/d) can prevent neuropathy.

items.title: Antiretroviral Agents and Peripheral Neuropathy: Mechanism and Management
items.content: Nucleoside analogues used in HIV treatment can cause dose-limiting sensory neuropathies. Zalcitabine, at doses >0.18 mg/kg per day, leads to severe burning pains due to mitochondrial DNA polymerase inhibition. Despite cessation of the drug, symptoms may worsen for 2–3 weeks (coasting effect) and improvement is seen after ~10 weeks post dose reduction.
keyPoints: Nucleoside analogues cause length-dependent sensory neuropathy
Zalcitabine >0.18 mg/kg/day associated with severe neuropathic pain
Mitochondrial DNA polymerase inhibition suspected pathogenic mechanism
Coasting effect: Symptoms worsen 2–3 weeks post cessation
Improvement seen after ~10 weeks following dose reduction

items.title: Hexacarbon Exposure and Neuropathy: Pathogenesis and Diagnosis
items.content: Exposure to hexacarbons like n-hexane and methyl n-butyl ketone leads to subacute sensory and motor polyneuropathy. This results from axonal neurofilament aggregation due to covalent cross-linking, impairing axonal transport and causing degeneration. NCS shows decreased SNAPs and CMAPs amplitudes with slightly slow conduction velocities.
keyPoints: Hexacarbon exposure causes sensory and motor polyneuropathy
Neurofilament aggregation due to covalent cross-linking leads to axonal degeneration
NCS: Decreased SNAPs and CMAPs amplitudes, slightly slow conduction velocities

items.title: Mercury Toxicity: Recognizing and Treating Neuropathy
items.content: Mercury toxicity can lead to a primarily axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy, with paresthesias progressing proximally from hands and feet. CNS symptoms often overshadow neuropathic signs, but motor weakness may also develop. The dorsal root ganglia are the primary site of neuromuscular pathology.
keyPoints: Mercury toxicity presents with paresthesias in hands and feet that progress proximally.
CNS symptoms can overshadow neuropathy; motor weakness may develop.
EDx shows features of a primarily axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy.
Primary site of pathology is the dorsal root ganglia.
Treatment involves removing the source of exposure.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · MERCURY | THALLIUM | ARSENIC

items.title: Thallium Toxicity: Clinical Features and Management
items.content: Thallium toxicity manifests with burning paresthesias, abdominal pain, vomiting, increased thirst, sleep disturbances, and psychotic behavior. Early signs include pigmentation changes and hyperreflexia, progressing to autonomic instability and hyporeflexia. Severe intoxication may lead to proximal weakness and respiratory muscle involvement requiring mechanical ventilation.
keyPoints: Thallium toxicity presents with burning paresthesias of the feet, abdominal pain, vomiting.
Early signs: pigmentation changes in hair, acne-like rash, hyperreflexia within the first week.
Autonomic instability and hyporeflexia may occur by weeks two to four.
Severe intoxication can cause proximal weakness and cranial nerve involvement.
Lethal dose ranges from 8 to 15 mg/kg body weight; death possible in <48 hours after large doses.
NCS show primarily axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy.
Potassium ferric ferrocyanide II may prevent absorption if given acutely.
Chelating agents are not very efficacious; adequate diuresis is essential.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · MERCURY | THALLIUM | ARSENIC

items.title: Identifying Cobalamin Deficiency in Peripheral Neuropathy
items.content: Cobalamin deficiency is a common cause of peripheral neuropathy, often presenting with numb hands before lower extremity paresthesias. It features large-fiber sensory loss affecting proprioception and vibration, sparing small-fiber modalities, leading to sensory ataxia. Hyperreflexia and absent Achilles reflexes are key indicators.
keyPoints: Pernicious anemia is the most common cause of cobalamin deficiency.
Numb hands typically appear before lower extremity paresthesias.
Large-fiber sensory loss affects proprioception and vibration, sparing small fibers.
Diffuse hyperreflexia and absent Achilles reflexes are indicative.
Up to 40% of patients may lack anemia and macrocytosis.
Serum methylmalonic acid and homocysteine levels are elevated in cobalamin deficiency.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · NUTRITIONAL NEUROPATHIES

items.title: Treatment Regimens for Cobalamin Deficiency
items.content: Cobalamin deficiency is treated with specific regimens, including 1000 µg cyanocobalamin IM weekly for one month and monthly thereafter. Patients with food cobalamin malabsorption can use oral supplementation of 1000 µg/d. Despite treatment, up to 50% may have permanent neurologic deficits.
keyPoints: Typical regimen: 1000 µg cyanocobalamin IM weekly for 1 month, then monthly.
Oral cobalamin dose of 1000 µg/d is sufficient for food cobalamin malabsorption.
Treatment does not completely reverse clinical manifestations.
At least 50% of patients exhibit some permanent neurologic deficit.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · NUTRITIONAL NEUROPATHIES

items.title: Recognizing Thiamine Deficiency in Peripheral Neuropathy
items.content: Thiamine deficiency, though uncommon in developed countries, can cause peripheral neuropathy. It is often linked to chronic alcohol abuse, recurrent vomiting, total parenteral nutrition, and bariatric surgery. Even healthy individuals on restrictive diets may be affected.
keyPoints: Thiamine (vitamin B1) is water-soluble and found in unrefined cereal grains, wheat germ, yeast, soybean flour, and pork.
Symptoms of neuropathy begin with mild sensory loss or burning dysesthesias in the toes and feet, progressing to generalized polyneuropathy.
Blood and urine assays for thiamine are unreliable; erythrocyte transketolase activity is more accurate.
Thiamine replacement should be administered intravenously or intramuscularly at 100 mg/d until proper nutrition is restored.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · THIAMINE DEFICIENCY

items.title: Management of Thiamine Deficiency and Its Neuropathic Effects
items.content: Upon suspicion or diagnosis of thiamine deficiency, immediate thiamine replacement is crucial. While cardiac symptoms respond well to treatment, neurological improvement can be variable.
keyPoints: Thiamine deficiency leads to dry beriberi (neuropathy) and wet beriberi (cardiac issues).
Neurological symptoms include distal sensory loss in feet and hands, with pain often being predominant.
Diagnosis relies on erythrocyte transketolase activity rather than blood/urine assays.
Thiamine is administered at 100 mg/d intravenously or intramuscularly until nutritional status improves.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · THIAMINE DEFICIENCY

items.title: Why Vitamin E Deficiency Mimics Spinocerebellar Ataxia
items.content: Vitamin E deficiency can present with clinical features similar to spinocerebellar ataxias, including progressive ataxia and posterior column dysfunction. This resemblance is due to the involvement of both polyneuropathy and spinal cord symptoms.
keyPoints: Deficiency typically results from lipid malabsorption or genetic disorders like abetalipoproteinemia.
Clinical features include spinocerebellar ataxia, polyneuropathy, impaired joint position, vibratory sensation, hyporeflexia, and extensor plantar responses.
Other neurological manifestations may involve ophthalmoplegia, pigmented retinopathy, night blindness, dysarthria, pseudoathetosis, dystonia, and tremor.
Diagnosis involves measuring serum $\alpha$-tocopherol levels; EDx shows axonal neuropathy features.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · VITAMIN E DEFICIENCY

items.title: How to Treat Vitamin E Deficiency in Isolated Cases
items.content: For isolated vitamin E deficiency, treatment involves oral supplementation with specific dosages. High doses are unnecessary, and routine serum testing for isolated polyneuropathy is not recommended.
keyPoints: Treatment consists of 1500–6000 IU/d of vitamin E in divided doses.
High-dose replacement is not required.
Serum vitamin E level checks have low yield in isolated polyneuropathy and are not part of routine practice.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · VITAMIN E DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: VITAMIN B. DEFICIENCY | PELLAGRA (NIACIN DEFICIENCY) | COPPER DEFICIENCY
items.title: Supplementation Strategies for Vitamin Deficiencies in Neuropathy
items.content: Understanding the appropriate supplementation strategies is crucial for managing neuropathies associated with vitamin deficiencies. For patients treated with isoniazid or hydralazine, vitamin B6 deficiency can be addressed by administering 50–100 mg/d of vitamin B6 to prevent or treat polyneuropathy. In cases of pellagra due to niacin deficiency, treatment involves niacin supplementation ranging from 40–250 mg/d.
keyPoints: Vitamin B6 deficiency is common in patients on isoniazid or hydralazine; treat with 50–100 mg/d of vitamin B6.
Pellagra due to niacin deficiency treated with 40–250 mg/d of niacin.
Neuropathy from these deficiencies can be nonspecific and generalized, affecting both central and peripheral nervous systems.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · VITAMIN B. DEFICIENCY | PELLAGRA (NIACIN DEFICIENCY) | COPPER DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: VITAMIN B. DEFICIENCY | PELLAGRA (NIACIN DEFICIENCY) | COPPER DEFICIENCY
items.title: Copper Deficiency Myeloneuropathy: Causes, Symptoms, and Treatment
items.content: Copper deficiency can lead to myeloneuropathy characterized by lower limb paresthesias, weakness, spasticity, and gait difficulties. The condition often results from factors like gastric surgery or excessive zinc intake, which inhibits copper absorption. Treatment involves oral copper sulfate or gluconate at 2 mg one to three times a day, with IV therapy as an alternative if oral supplementation is ineffective.
keyPoints: Copper deficiency myeloneuropathy presents with lower limb paresthesias, weakness, spasticity, and gait difficulties.
Causes include gastric surgery, excessive zinc intake, malnutrition, and copper-chelating agents.
Treatment: Oral copper sulfate or gluconate 2 mg one to three times daily; IV therapy if oral is ineffective.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · VITAMIN B. DEFICIENCY | PELLAGRA (NIACIN DEFICIENCY) | COPPER DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHY ASSOCIATED WITH GASTRIC SURGERY | CRYPTOGENIC (IDIOPATHIC) SENSORY AND SENSORIMOTOR POLYNEUROPATHY
items.title: Polyneuropathy Post-Gastric Surgery: Mechanisms and Management
items.content: Polyneuropathy can occur after gastric surgeries such as gastrojejunostomy, gastric stapling, vertical banded gastroplasty, or gastrectomy with Roux-en-Y anastomosis. This typically happens in the context of rapid weight loss and recurrent vomiting, manifesting as acute or subacute sensory loss and weakness within months post-surgery. Management includes parenteral vitamin supplementation, notably thiamine, which can lead to improvement.
keyPoints: Polyneuropathy may occur following gastric surgery for ulcer, cancer, or weight reduction.
Manifestations include numbness and paresthesias in the feet (Pattern 2).
Management includes parenteral vitamin supplementation, especially thiamine.
Duration and severity of deficits before treatment are predictors of outcome.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · NEUROPATHY ASSOCIATED WITH GASTRIC SURGERY | CRYPTOGENIC (IDIOPATHIC) SENSORY AND SENSORIMOTOR POLYNEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHY ASSOCIATED WITH GASTRIC SURGERY | CRYPTOGENIC (IDIOPATHIC) SENSORY AND SENSORIMOTOR POLYNEUROPATHY
items.title: Characteristics and Management of Cryptogenic Sensory and Sensorimotor Polyneuropathy
items.content: Cryptogenic sensory and sensorimotor polyneuropathy (CSPN) is a diagnosis of exclusion, often presenting in the sixth and seventh decades with distal numbness, tingling, and burning pain starting in the feet. It involves both large- and small-fiber loss, with absent ankle reflexes being common. Management focuses on controlling neuropathic pain using nortriptyline or duloxetine, which are more effective than pregabalin or mexiletine.
keyPoints: CSPN is a diagnosis of exclusion after extensive evaluation.
Onset predominantly occurs in the sixth and seventh decades.
Patients exhibit distal sensory loss to pinprick, touch, and vibration (Pattern 2).
Approximately 10% have only small-fiber involvement.
Nortriptyline and duloxetine are preferred for neuropathic pain management over pregabalin and mexiletine.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · NEUROPATHY ASSOCIATED WITH GASTRIC SURGERY | CRYPTOGENIC (IDIOPATHIC) SENSORY AND SENSORIMOTOR POLYNEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: MEDIAN NEUROPATHY | ULNAR NEUROPATHY AT THE ELBOW—“CUBITAL TUNNEL SYNDROME”
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Carpal Tunnel Syndrome (CTS)
items.content: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome is characterized by compression of the median nerve at the wrist, leading to numbness, paresthesias in specific fingers, pain, and weakness. Diagnosis involves clinical signs such as Tinel's and Phalen's tests, along with electrodiagnostic studies showing slowed sensory and motor potentials across the wrist.
keyPoints: Symptoms: Numbness and paresthesias in thumb, index, middle, and half of ring finger; pain in hand, forearm, or proximal arm.
Signs: Decreased sensation in median nerve distribution; positive Tinel's sign (percussion over wrist); positive Phalen's test (wrist flexed for 30–60 s).
Electrodiagnostic findings: Slowing of sensory and motor median potentials across the wrist.

Treatment options: Avoidance of precipitating activities, NSAIDs, wrist splints, glucocorticoid/anesthetic injection, surgical decompression if poor response to nonsurgical treatments or presence of thenar muscle atrophy/weakness.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · MEDIAN NEUROPATHY | ULNAR NEUROPATHY AT THE ELBOW—“CUBITAL TUNNEL SYNDROME”

items.subtopic: MEDIAN NEUROPATHY | ULNAR NEUROPATHY AT THE ELBOW—“CUBITAL TUNNEL SYNDROME”
items.title: Recognition and Treatment of Cubital Tunnel Syndrome
items.content: Cubital Tunnel Syndrome involves ulnar nerve compression at the elbow, presenting with paresthesias in specific fingers, pain, and muscle weakness. Diagnosis includes clinical signs such as decreased sensation in an ulnar distribution, positive Tinel's sign at the elbow, and Froment's sign indicating thumb adductor weakness.
keyPoints: Symptoms: Paresthesias, tingling, numbness in medial hand and half of fourth and entire fifth fingers; pain at elbow or forearm; muscle weakness.
Signs: Decreased sensation in ulnar distribution; positive Tinel's sign at the elbow; Froment's sign (thumb adductor weakness).
Electrodiagnostic findings: Slowing of ulnar motor nerve conduction velocity across the elbow with prolonged sensory latencies.
Treatment options: Avoid aggravating factors, use elbow pads, surgery to decompress the nerve in the cubital tunnel if necessary.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · MEDIAN NEUROPATHY | ULNAR NEUROPATHY AT THE ELBOW—“CUBITAL TUNNEL SYNDROME”

items.subtopic: RADIAL NEUROPATHY | LATERAL FEMORAL CUTANEOUS NEUROPATHY (MERALGIA PARESTHETICA)
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Radial Neuropathy
items.content: Radial neuropathy often presents with wrist drop, finger extension weakness, thumb abduction weakness, and sensory loss in the dorsal web between the thumb and index finger. Most cases are transient compressive injuries that recover spontaneously within 6–8 weeks. If no improvement is observed in 2–3 weeks, an electrodiagnostic (EDx) study should be conducted to confirm the diagnosis and assess severity.
keyPoints: Symptoms: wrist drop, finger extension weakness, thumb abduction weakness, sensory loss in dorsal web
Triceps and brachioradialis strength often normal; triceps reflex intact
Recovery time for transient compressive injuries: 6–8 weeks
If no improvement in 2–3 weeks, conduct EDx study
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · RADIAL NEUROPATHY | LATERAL FEMORAL CUTANEOUS NEUROPATHY (MERALGIA PARESTHETICA)

items.subtopic: RADIAL NEUROPATHY | LATERAL FEMORAL CUTANEOUS NEUROPATHY (MERALGIA PARESTHETICA)
items.title: Clinical Approach to Meralgia Paresthetica
items.content: Meralgia paresthetica presents with lateral thigh paresthesias, numbness, and occasional pain, exacerbated by standing or walking. Diagnosis is clinical; EDx is only needed if lumbar plexopathy, radiculopathy, or femoral neuropathy must be ruled out. Symptoms often resolve spontaneously over weeks to months but may leave permanent numbness.
keyPoints: Symptoms: lateral thigh paresthesias, numbness, pain; increased by standing/walking
Diagnosis is clinical; EDx only if ruling out other neuropathies
Spontaneous resolution often occurs over weeks to months
Treatment includes weight loss, avoiding tight belts, and analgesics like lidocaine patches
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · RADIAL NEUROPATHY | LATERAL FEMORAL CUTANEOUS NEUROPATHY (MERALGIA PARESTHETICA)

items.subtopic: FEMORAL NEUROPATHY | SCIATIC NEUROPATHY | PERONEAL NEUROPATHY
items.title: Identifying Causes and Symptoms of Femoral Neuropathy
items.content: Femoral neuropathy can result from various conditions such as retroperitoneal hematoma, lithotomy positioning, hip arthroplasty or dislocation, iliac artery occlusion, femoral arterial procedures, infiltration by hematogenous malignancy, penetrating groin trauma, pelvic surgery including hysterectomy and renal transplantation, and diabetes. Patients typically exhibit difficulty in extending the knee and flexing the hip, with sensory symptoms on the anterior thigh and/or medial leg occurring in only half of cases.
keyPoints: Causes include retroperitoneal hematoma, lithotomy positioning, hip arthroplasty or dislocation, iliac artery occlusion, femoral arterial procedures, infiltration by hematogenous malignancy, penetrating groin trauma, pelvic surgery (hysterectomy, renal transplantation), and diabetes.
Symptoms: difficulty extending knee and flexing hip; sensory symptoms in anterior thigh/medial leg occur in 50% of cases.
Quadriceps (patellar) reflex is diminished.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · FEMORAL NEUROPATHY | SCIATIC NEUROPATHY | PERONEAL NEUROPATHY

items.subtopic: FEMORAL NEUROPATHY | SCIATIC NEUROPATHY | PERONEAL NEUROPATHY
items.title: Distinguishing Features and Management of Peroneal Neuropathy
items.content: Peroneal neuropathy is characterized by foot drop, which includes weakness in ankle dorsiflexion, toe extension, and ankle eversion. Sensory loss may follow the superficial or deep peroneal pattern without pain. It can be differentiated from L5 radiculopathy through EMG findings and nerve conduction studies showing slowing across the fibular head. Management involves rapid weight loss, avoiding leg crossing, using an ankle brace for foot drop, and a knee pad to prevent further compression.
keyPoints: Symptoms: foot drop (ankle dorsiflexion, toe extension, ankle eversion weakness) with variable sensory loss; usually no pain.
Differentiation from L5 radiculopathy: EMG shows denervation in L5, peroneal neuropathy shows slowing and amplitude drop across fibular head.
Management: rapid weight loss, avoid leg crossing, use ankle brace for foot drop, knee pad to prevent compression.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · FEMORAL NEUROPATHY | SCIATIC NEUROPATHY | PERONEAL NEUROPATHY

items.title: Acute Brachial Plexus Neuropathy Presentation and Progression
items.content: Immune-mediated brachial plexus neuropathy (IBPN) presents with acute onset of severe shoulder pain, which can last from several days to a few weeks. As the intense pain subsides, weakness and sensory loss become apparent due to limited early movement.
keyPoints: IBPN is also known as acute brachial plexitis or Parsonage-Turner syndrome.
Severe shoulder pain with an acute onset characterizes IBPN.
Pain duration: several days to a few weeks, followed by persistent dull ache.
Weakness and sensory loss become evident after the initial pain phase.
Recurrent attacks are possible.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · RADICULOPATHIES | BRACHIAL PLEXUS

items.title: Anatomy of the Brachial Plexus
items.content: The brachial plexus consists of three trunks (upper, middle, lower), each with two divisions (anterior and posterior). These trunks further divide into three cords (medial, lateral, posterior) that give rise to terminal nerves innervating the arm.
keyPoints: The brachial plexus comprises three trunks: upper, middle, and lower.
Each trunk has two divisions: anterior and posterior.
Trunks divide into three cords: medial, lateral, and posterior.
Terminal nerves from these cords innervate the arm.
Upper trunk formed by fusion of C5 and C6 rami.
Middle trunk is the continuation of C7 ramus.
Lower trunk formed by joining of C8 and T1 rami.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · RADICULOPATHIES | BRACHIAL PLEXUS

items.title: Pancoast Tumor Presentation and Imaging
items.content: A Pancoast tumor, typically arising from the upper lobe of the lung, can invade or compress the lower trunk of the brachial plexus. It presents with insidious onset pain in the upper arm, sensory disturbance in the medial forearm and hand, weakness and atrophy of intrinsic hand muscles, and ipsilateral Horner's syndrome.
keyPoints: Pancoast tumors present with specific symptoms: upper arm pain, medial forearm/hand sensory disturbance, intrinsic hand muscle weakness/atrophy, and ipsilateral Horner's syndrome.
Chest CT or MRI can demonstrate tumor extension into the brachial plexus.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · TABLE 457-9 Causes of Radiculopathy

items.title: Brachial Plexopathy Post-Median Sternotomy
items.content: Median sternotomy, often performed during open-heart surgeries, can lead to brachial plexopathy in up to 5% of patients. This typically affects the lower trunk, causing sensory disturbance and weakness in the medial forearm and hand.
keyPoints: Brachial plexopathy occurs in as many as 5% of patients following median sternotomy.
The condition usually involves the lower trunk, leading to sensory disturbances and intrinsic hand muscle weakness.
Most individuals recover within a few months due to stretch-related mechanisms.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · TABLE 457-9 Causes of Radiculopathy

items.title: Lumbosacral Plexus Anatomy and Nerve Derivation
items.content: The lumbar plexus arises from the ventral primary rami of L1 to L4, with the femoral nerve deriving from L2-L4 dorsal branches. The sacral plexus forms by union of the lumbosacral trunk (L4-S5) and S1-S4 ventral rami.
keyPoints: The lumbar plexus arises from L1 to L4 ventral primary rami, with the femoral nerve from L2-L4 dorsal branches.
The sacral plexus forms by union of lumbosacral trunk (L4-S5) and S1-S4 ventral rami.
Sciatic nerve lateral trunk (common peroneal nerve) arises from L4, L5, S1, S2 dorsal branches; medial trunk (tibial nerve) from L4-S2 ventral branches.
items.reference: Peripheral Neuropathy · TABLE 457-9 Causes of Radiculopathy

items.subtopic: LUMBOSACRAL PLEXOPATHIES | RECURRENT NEOPLASTIC DISEASE OR RADIATION-INDUCED PLEXOPATHY | EVALUATION AND TREATMENT OF PLEXOPATHIES
items.title: Differentiating Radiation-Induced from Neoplastic Plexopathy
items.content: Radiation-induced plexopathies typically develop months or years post-radiation therapy and are dose-dependent, often presenting without pain and affecting the upper trunk. In contrast, neoplastic invasion is usually painful and more commonly affects the lower trunk.
keyPoints: Radiation-induced plexopathy can occur months to years after radiation therapy and is dose dependent.
Radiation injury is often painless and affects the upper trunk.
Tumor invasion tends to be painful and affects the lower trunk.
EMG showing myokymic discharges suggests radiation-induced damage.

items.title: Initial Management of Acute Idiopathic Lumbosacral Plexopathy
items.content: In cases of severe pain from acute idiopathic lumbosacral plexopathy, a short course of glucocorticoids may be effective in managing symptoms.
keyPoints: Severe pain from acute idiopathic lumbosacral plexopathy can respond to glucocorticoids.
Glucocorticoid treatment is typically administered as a short course.
